channel.title: NYT > World News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: China Confirms Death of Pilot Who Flew Small Plane Into Beijing’s Tallest Tower
item.description: The statement came a day after online images showed falling debris and plane parts. Another 13 were injured in the incident five miles from Communist Party headquarters.
item.dc:creator: Lily Kuo, Pei-Lin Wu and Keith Bradsher
item.media:credit: Keith Bradsher/The New York Times
item.media:description: A hole was still visible on Sunday morning on the eastern face of Citic Tower in Beijing, after a light plane hit it on Friday.

item.title: A Mayor Is Taking Maternity Leave in Japan. Some Men Are Furious.
item.description: Shoko Kawata is the first mayor to take maternity leave in Japan’s history. Her decision has prompted debate about gender discrimination in the work force.
item.dc:creator: Javier C. Hernández and Kiuko Notoya
item.media:credit: Noriko Hayashi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Shoko Kawata, the mayor of Yawata, in her office this month. She will be the first Japanese mayor to take maternity leave.

item.title: Drones Used to Fight Mosquitoes as Dengue Fever Surges in Sri Lanka
item.description: The military has joined the battle against the insects, as an outbreak strains hospitals in a country already reeling from energy shortages caused by the Iran War.
item.dc:creator: Pamodi Waravita
item.media:credit: Ishara S. Kodikara/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: A municipal worker fumigating on Wednesday during a nationwide three-day drive to eliminate breeding sites for the dengue-carrying Aedes mosquito in Colombo, Sri Lanka.

item.title: Volunteers Are Risking Their Lives to Stop Ebola. They Aren’t Always Welcome.
item.description: Frontline Congolese workers who conduct safe burials are key to containing what experts fear could become the worst ever Ebola outbreak. They risk not just infection, but the anger of terrified communities.
item.dc:creator: Declan Walsh and Arlette Bashizi
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times

item.title: Chaotic Rush to Help Venezuela Earthquake Victims Delays Some Rescues
item.description: A surge of people rushed into the devastated earthquake zone to offer help in northern Venezuela, slowing the advance of emergency responders. The death toll linked to twin quakes earlier in the week surpassed 1,400.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times

item.title: Mexican Officials Have Become Informants for the Trump Administration
item.description: President Claudia Sheinbaum of Mexico has pushed back against U.S. investigations into Mexican politicians. Now some politicians want to cooperate.
item.dc:creator: Steve Fisher, Jack Nicas and Alan Feuer
item.media:credit: Luis Antonio Rojas for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Claudia Sheinbaum of Mexico, seen here at the Supreme Court of Justice in Mexico last year, has objected to U.S. investigations into officials from her political party.

item.title: U.S. and Iran Trade More Strikes, Testing Truce
item.description: U.S. forces carried out more airstrikes on Iran, calling them a retaliation for an attack on an oil tanker. Iran’s military later said it had attacked American targets in Bahrain and Kuwait.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times

item.title: Australia Says It Will Toughen Its Social Media Ban for Children
item.description: Saying tech companies were “not doing enough” to keep youths under 16 off their platforms, the government announced tougher fines and new powers for a regulator.
item.dc:creator: Victoria Kim
item.media:credit: Matthew Abbott for The New York Times
item.media:description: Most young Australian teenagers are still using major social media platforms despite the ban that took effect in December, researchers have found.

item.title: Once He Wanted to Lead FIFA. This Is Much Better.
item.description: Jordan’s arrival at the World Cup is the culmination of decades of patient effort by one man, but also a moment of solidarity for his nation and its diaspora.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja
item.media:credit: Hazem Bader/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Prince Ali bin Al Hussein, center, ahead of a soccer game between the Jordanian and Palestinian teams in 2023.

item.title: Venezuela’s Economy Was On the Rise. Then the Earthquakes Struck.
item.description: Expectations are now rising over the role the United States will play in helping Venezuela, which had already been laid low by decades of misrule.
item.dc:creator: Simon Romero and Emma Bubola
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: A residential building that collapsed during back-to-back earthquakes in La Guaira, Venezuela, on Thursday.

item.title: After New York Elections, Israelis Fear Further Rupture in U.S.-Israeli Relationship
item.description: Criticism of Israel’s war in Gaza, anger over the Iran war and election results in New York all suggest Israel’s solid support from Washington may be on borrowed time.
item.dc:creator: David M. Halbfinger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Angelina Katsanis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani of New York, center, in Manhattan on Tuesday. Three pro-Palestinian candidates backed by Mr. Mamdani, an ardent critic of Israel, defeated moderates in Democratic congressional primaries this week.

item.title: Central and Eastern Europe Are Feeling the Heat Now
item.description: Much of Germany and Poland were under extreme heat warnings on Saturday as the weather phenomenon driving this week’s record-breaking temperatures moved east.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel
item.media:credit: Henning Kaiser/Dpa, via Associated Press
item.media:description: A resident of a nursing home in Dormagen, Germany, was put into an ambulance on Saturday as the facility was evacuated because of the heat.

item.title: Exiled Chefs From Myanmar Give Burmese Food Chance to Shine on International Stage
item.description: Much of the world is unfamiliar with the cuisine, but an exodus from Myanmar after a coup has made it more visible on the international stage.
item.dc:creator: Verena Hölzl and Lauren DeCicca
item.category: Coups D'Etat and Attempted Coups D'Etat
item.media:credit: Lauren DeCicca for The New York Times

item.title: A Sculptor Rushes to Finish a Lionel Messi Statue During the World Cup
item.description: As Lionel Messi chases another World Cup title, an artist is rushing to finish a gigantic statue of the soccer star in the middle of the Patagonian desert.
item.dc:creator: Emma Bubola and Sarah Pabst
item.media:credit: Sarah Pabst for The New York Times
item.media:description: The giant statue of Lionel Messi made by Aldo Beroisa in the remote oil town of Cutral Có, Argentina.

item.title: Zelensky Steps Up Threats Against Belarus for Aiding Drone Attacks
item.description: Ukrainian officials say their northern neighbor is allowing its radio relay stations to be used to guide Russian attack drones more precisely.
item.dc:creator: Carlotta Gall and Oleksandr Chubko
item.media:credit: Genya Savilov/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: A destroyed bridge over the Dnipro River, which forms the border between Ukraine and Belarus, in Chernihiv region, Ukraine, this month.

item.title: A $2.5 Billion Whodunit: The Hack That Dented the U.K. Economy
item.description: A loose collective of cybercriminals initially took credit for crippling Jaguar Land Rover last year. Investigators now see Russian hands behind the ransomware attack.
item.dc:creator: Adam Goldman, Jane Bradley, Dustin Volz and Michael Schwirtz
item.media:credit: Andrew Testa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Land Rovers being transported from the Jaguar Land Rover factory in Solihull, England, last year.

item.title: Small Plane Crashes Into Tallest Building in Beijing
item.description: Crowds in the capital’s central business district fled as the crash sent debris falling into the streets below.
item.dc:creator: Keith Bradsher, Meaghan Tobin and Pei-Lin Wu
item.media:credit: Maxim Shemetov/Reuters
item.media:description: The damaged exterior of Citic Tower, also known as China Zun, in Beijing, China on Friday.

item.title: U.S. Officials Said to Be Frustrated With Machado’s Call for Help
item.description: María Corina Machado, the exiled leader of the Venezuelan opposition, hopes to go home. U.S. officials say her wishes to do so come at an inopportune time.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Civilians searching for survivors in a collapsed residential building in La Guaira, Venezuela, on Thursday.

item.title: A Rush of Volunteers in Venezuela Has Slowed Rescue Efforts
item.description: The outpouring of volunteer aid after Venezuela’s earthquakes clogged the only road into the disaster zone, delaying rescue crews.
item.dc:creator: Genevieve Glatsky, María Victoria Fermín and Fabiola Ferrero
item.media:credit: Fabiola Ferrero for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Pérez de León Hospital medical team in a truck in La Guaira, Venezuela, on Friday night. The doctors were handing out food, water and medical supplies to people on the streets.

item.title: 12 Hours With Venezuelan Doctors Searching for Earthquake Survivors
item.description: A medical team set out from the capital to rescue people in the hardest-hit part of the disaster zone, La Guaira. They found silence in the ruins instead.
item.dc:creator: Fabiola Ferrero and Julie Turkewitz
item.media:credit: Fabiola Ferrero for The New York Times
item.media:description: Dr. Zaira Medina, left, hugging a rescue worker outside her collapsed apartment building in La Guaira, Venezuela, on Friday.

item.title: Foreign rescuers in Venezuela battle debris, scarcity of supplies and time.
item.dc:creator: Ishani Desai and Jonathan Wolfe
item.media:credit: Fabiola Ferrero for The New York Times
item.media:description: Members of a Colombian search-and-rescue team working among collapsed buildings in La Guaira, Venezuela, on Friday.

item.title: A Close Call With an Alberta Grizzly Bear, Recorded in Its Entirety
item.description: A woman in Alberta, Canada, recorded the bear as it circled her and her dog near a campsite and lodge.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.media:credit: Artur Widak/NurPhoto, via Getty Images

item.title: In the Ruins of Venezuela’s Earthquake, Civilians Volunteers Fill the Gaps
item.description: Volunteers have rushed to help earthquake victims, loading vehicles with medical supplies, shovels and other tools to assist rescue and recovery efforts.
item.dc:creator: Genevieve Glatsky
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Civilians and trained emergency workers looked for survivors in La Guaira, Venezuela, on Thursday. La Guaira was the state hit hardest by the earthquakes.

item.title: The scale of destruction heralds a larger death toll to come, a top U.N. official says.
item.dc:creator: Julie Turkewitz
item.media:credit: Fabiola Ferrero for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rescue operations at a building in La Guaira, Venezuela, on Friday.

item.title: Deal With Israel Divides Lebanese, Fueling Protests in Beirut
item.description: Supporters welcomed the agreement as a step toward curtailing Iran’s influence within Lebanon, but others took to the streets, calling it a capitulation.
item.dc:creator: Abdi Latif Dahir
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Ibrahim Amro/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: People in Beirut protesting against the agreement that was signed late Friday between Israel, Lebanon and the United States.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Wolfe, Isayen Herrera, María Victoria Fermín, Julie Turkewitz, Genevieve Glatsky and Fabiola Ferrero

item.title: U.S.-Iran Deal’s Vague Language Comes Back to Haunt Peace Efforts
item.description: The deal called for Iran to “make arrangements” for the passage of ships through the Strait of Hormuz. Iran has interpreted that to mean it can designate which routes ships take.
item.dc:creator: Yeganeh Torbati
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Reuters
item.media:description: Vessels in the Strait of Hormuz, as seen from Oman. Iran on Thursday struck a container ship passing through the waterway.

item.title: Chinese Dissident Who Fled by Sea to South Korea Lands in Canada
item.description: Dong Guangping, who reached Toronto on Friday after a decade of failed attempts to escape China, recounted how he traveled by sea to South Korea last month.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon and Pei-Lin Wu

item.title: Natural Selection
item.description: Sometimes, our preference for certitude, our need to define things with conviction, closes us off from possibility.
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch

item.title: Affogato and Rendang on the Menu in Canada’s High Arctic
item.description: The menu offerings at Kuugaq Café, owned by immigrants from the Philippines, speaks to the increasing diversity of Cambridge Bay, Nunavut.
item.dc:creator: Norimitsu Onishi
item.media:credit: Renaud Philippe for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Inuit hamlet of Cambridge Bay on Victoria Island, Nunavut, in the middle of February during a week temperatures reached minus 60 degrees Celsius with the wind chill. It’s much warmer this time of year.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman, Euan Ward, Ismaeel Naar and Farnaz Fassihi

item.title: An Attack on Ebola’s First Responders
item.description: Our chief Africa correspondent, Declan Walsh, reports from a city where Red Cross workers carrying out burials in the Ebola outbreak have come under attack.
item.dc:creator: Declan Walsh, Estelle Caswell, Coleman Lowndes, Thomas Vollkommer and Arlette Bashizi

item.title: At the Stadium and Far Away, Cape Verde Fans Embrace the Possible
item.description: Cape Verdeans were ecstatic when their country qualified for the World Cup. Their joy has now been supercharged by what seemed unthinkable before the tournament: a spot in the knockouts.
item.dc:creator: Mitch Smith and Madaleine Rubin
item.media:credit: Desiree Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Fans and players celebrated Cape Verde’s draw with Saudi Arabia.

item.title: Nations Send Rescue Teams and Aid to Venezuela After Earthquakes
item.description: The number of the dead continues to rise as search-and-rescue teams descended upon the country to dig out people buried in rubble.
item.dc:creator: Ishani Desai
item.media:credit: Alexandre Meneghini/Reuters
item.media:description: A search and rescue team from Brazil boards an aircraft on the way to aid efforts in Venezuela, in São Paulo, on Friday.

item.title: Convicted Rapist Who Fled to Scotland and Faked His Own Death Dies in Utah
item.description: Nicholas Rossi, 38, raped two girlfriends in 2008 and later fled to Scotland, prosecutors said. An attentive nurse treating him for Covid in 2021 identified him.
item.dc:creator: Mack Liederman
item.media:credit: Jeff J Mitchell/Getty Images
item.media:description: Nicholas Rossi, who fled the United States and was accused of faking his own death before being convicted of rape charges, died in prison.

item.title: Scenes of Collapse: The Emergency at Venezuela’s Hospitals
item.description: A snapshot of the toll of the country’s twin quakes.
item.dc:creator: Isayen Herrera and Julie Turkewitz
item.media:credit: Fabiola Ferrero for The New York Times
item.media:description: Many of the hospitals and clinics left standing after Venezuela’s back-to-back earthquakes have been overwhelmed.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Helene Cooper, Euan Ward, Jenny Gross, Pranav Baskar and Alan Yuhas

item.title: As Foreign Aid Arrived, Many Venezuelans Asked: ‘Where is Our Military?’
item.description: The nation’s armed forces seemed to arrive late into the emergency response — with an emphasis on controlling traffic, even as bodies remained buried under rubble.
item.dc:creator: Zane Irwin
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Members of the Bolivarian National Guard of Venezuela searching for survivors in Pinto Salinas, Venezuela, on Wednesday after two earthquakes hit the country earlier that afternoon.

item.title: A floating convenience store appears in Toronto
item.description: “Global Convenience,” an art installation honoring the city’s role as a World Cup host, is a near-perfect replica of a corner shop. Someone got stranded after swimming out to it last week.
item.dc:creator: John S.W. MacDonald
item.media:credit: Trevor Wheatley, Cosmo Dean, and Puncture Design

item.title: In Venezuela’s Rubble, Listening for Whispers And Longing for Help
item.description: Facing criticism from residents that it was not doing enough to help, the Venezuelan government said it had dispatched more than 100 heavy machines to clear debris.
item.dc:creator: Fabiola Ferrero, Julie Turkewitz, Luis Ferré-Sadurní and Alan Yuhas
item.media:credit: Fabiola Ferrero for The New York Times
item.media:description: A survivor on Friday. About 1,400 buildings had been damaged, including 13 hospitals and 25 shopping centers.

item.title: Migrant Camps Swell in South Africa Amid Growing Anti-Immigrant Threats
item.description: A threat to all undocumented foreigners to leave the country by Tuesday has led many to set up street camps as they try to get out.
item.dc:creator: John Eligon and Joao Silva
item.media:credit: Joao Silva/The New York Times

item.title: Why Lebanon Is Celebrating Brazil’s Win
item.description: Our correspondent Abdi Latif Dahir visits a Brazil national team World Cup watch party in Tripoli, Lebanon. Brazil is especially popular here as it’s home to the world’s largest Lebanese diaspora.
item.dc:creator: Abdi Latif Dahir, Christina Thornell and Coleman Lowndes

item.title: Earthquake Tests Growing Ties Between U.S. and Venezuela
item.description: The Trump administration said it would commit aid, at a time when it has been expanding U.S. commercial interests in Venezuela beyond oil.
item.dc:creator: Edward Wong and Michael Crowley
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rescue workers and civilians looking for people trapped under debris in La Guaira, Venezuela, after two large earthquakes.

item.title: Yves Lacoste, Who Exposed U.S. Bombing of Vietnam’s Waterways, Dies at 96
item.description: He used a geographer’s tools to demonstrate that North Vietnamese civilian infrastructure had been deliberately attacked, threatening millions of lives.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nossiter
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Louis Monier/Gamma-Rapho, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Yves Lacoste in 1992. The month after he had published his findings, the U.S. halted Operation Linebacker, a massive bombing campaign on the Red River Delta in North Vietnam.

item.title: Search Teams From California, Virginia and Florida Head to Venezuela
item.description: The groups that include firefighters, doctors and structural engineering specialists began arriving on Friday to join the race to find survivors.
item.dc:creator: Billy Witz and Shawn Hubler
item.media:credit: Blake Fagan/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Members of the County of Los Angeles search and rescue team on Thursday before leaving for Venezuela.

item.title: The Venezuela Earthquakes Hit a Health System Already in Crisis
item.description: Firefighters are using cellphone lights because of a flashlight shortage, and an overwhelmed hospital in the disaster zone is operating without running water, one doctor said.
item.dc:creator: Genevieve Glatsky
item.media:credit: Fabiola Ferrero for The New York Times
item.media:description: A woman injured in Wednesday’s earthquakes was loaded into a van to be transported from a hospital in La Guaira, Venezuela, to a hospital in Caracas, the capital, on Thursday.

item.title: The Netherlands Records Its First Assisted Death of a Child Between 1 and 12
item.description: Physician-assisted death for terminally ill children in that age group has been allowed in the country since 2024.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses
item.media:credit: Nicolas Tucat/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Sophie Hermans, the minister of health, welfare and sport in the Netherlands, in 2025.

item.title: The Moment One Venezuelan Survivor Was Pulled From a Flattened Building
item.description: Graciela Mora was with a friend when earthquakes hit northern Venezuela. Video captured the moment she was rescued.
item.dc:creator: Max Bearak

item.title: Survivors in quake-hit La Guaira tell of loss and rescue.
item.dc:creator: Fabiola Ferrero and Jonathan Wolfe
item.media:credit: Fabiola Ferrero for The New York Times
item.media:description: Juan David Arsia, 17, on a stretcher outside the emergency entrance of a small hospital in La Guaira, Venezuela, on Thursday.

item.title: Venezuelans Displaced by Quakes Sleep in Streets, Plazas and Cars
item.description: Twin earthquakes have left thousands homeless. Many others who are too afraid to sleep in their homes have taken to the streets.
item.dc:creator: Luis Ferré-Sadurní and María Victoria Fermín
item.media:credit: Fabiola Ferrero for The New York Times
item.media:description: Displaced residents at a stadium in La Guaira, Venezuela, on Thursday.

item.title: Canada’s 24 Sussex Drive Is a Dump. Mark Carney Is Going to Fix It Up.
item.description: The prime minister’s official residence in Ottawa, in disrepair and vacant for more than a decade, has become the country’s most prominent home renovation project.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:credit: Ian Austen/The New York Times
item.media:description: 24 Sussex Drive, the official residence of Canada’s prime ministers.

item.title: Psychiatrist Who Killed Six at German Christmas Market Is Jailed for Life
item.description: The man drove into a crowd of shoppers in December 2024, wounding hundreds of people.
item.dc:creator: Christopher F. Schuetze
item.media:credit: Fabrizio Bensch/Reuters
item.media:description: The defendant, whom the court identified only as Taleb A. to comply with privacy law, during his hearing in Magdeburg, Germany, on Friday.

item.title: Ukrainian Attacks Spur State of Emergency Declaration in Crimea
item.description: Weeks of intense strikes by Ukraine have rattled everyday life in Crimea to an extent unseen since Russia illegally annexed the peninsula in 2014.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Nechepurenko and Nataliya Vasilyeva
item.media:credit: Vantor, via Reuters
item.media:description: A satellite image from Sunday, provided by the company Vantor, shows damage to the Henichesk Bridge, which links Crimea to the rest of Ukraine.

item.title: U.S. Strikes Iran in Retaliation for Attack on Vessel in Strait of Hormuz
item.description: President Trump on Friday called Iran’s attack on a container ship transiting the Strait of Hormuz a day earlier a “foolish” act.
item.dc:creator: Helene Cooper, Euan Ward, Jenny Gross and Pranav Baskar
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Elke Scholiers/Getty Images
item.media:description: Oil tankers and cargo vessels anchored off the coast of Oman this week.

item.title: Ryanair Stops Charging for Parents to Sit With Their Children
item.description: The airline said it had “reluctantly” changed course after a British regulator began investigating whether its fees were illegal.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon
item.media:credit: Adrian Dennis/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Ryanair was the only major airline operating in Britain that imposed this type of fee, according to a regulator.

item.title: As Europe Sweats, Some Politicians Talk of Air-Conditioning, Not Climate Action
item.description: Heat-related deaths and disruptions to daily life are forcing politicians to reckon, in different ways, with a rapidly warming planet.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear and Jeanna Smialek
item.media:credit: Dmitry Kostyukov for The New York Times
item.media:description: Carrying a floor fan during the heat in Paris on Thursday.

item.title: Heat Wave Prompts Paris to Suspend Sports Events and Public Drinking
item.description: The Paris police chief, Patrice Faure, said that alcohol restrictions were intended to ease pressure on health services.
item.dc:creator: Ségolène Le Stradic
item.media:credit: Ludovic Marin/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Paris is temporarily banning alcohol consumption in streets and parks.
